one_line_takeaway: A synthesis survey -- not a new attack or defense -- that organizes existing backdoor attacks and defenses on contrastive learning into a four-dimension taxonomy and five comparison tables, concluding that representation-level backdoors resist label-based defenses and that sensor/time-series federated contrastive learning remains an unaddressed attack surface.
key_finding: The survey's substantive claim, drawn from and attributed to the works it reviews, is that contrastive learning is disproportionately vulnerable to backdoors relative to supervised learning: extremely low poisoning ratios (per Carlini & Terzis [7], <0.1% of training data) suffice to implant a backdoor because contrastive objectives bind trigger representations to target concepts in embedding space rather than to output labels, and this compromise transfers across downstream tasks, architectures, and (in the federated case, per BAGEL [11]) across the aggregated global encoder -- all while causing negligible degradation on clean-data performance per the works surveyed. The survey's own synthesis is that this representation-level, cross-task-transferable character of contrastive backdoors, per the authors' analysis in Tables I-V, causes standard label-based and gradient-anomaly defenses to fail, and that even the two multimodal defenses reviewed in depth (CleanCLIP, CleanerCLIP) trade off overhead against robustness to adaptive attacks rather than solving the problem: CleanCLIP's pair-level similarity filtering is described as bypassable by adaptive/semantically-plausible triggers, while CleanerCLIP's representation-level counterfactual augmentation is described as more robust but computationally heavier and dependent on semantic-decomposition quality. The authors identify, as a gap not covered by any reviewed work, backdoor attacks/defenses for sensor and time-series contrastive learning in federated/industrial IoT settings.
core_contribution: This is a survey/review paper, not a new-attack or new-defense paper. Its own contribution is: (1) a four-dimensional taxonomy for classifying backdoor attacks in contrastive learning (attack stage: centralized vs. federated; trigger type: static patch, semantic, multimodal association; target domain: vision, multimodal, graph, time-series/industrial IoT; attack objective: downstream misclassification, representation hijacking, cross-task transfer); (2) five comparative tables (Tables I-V) synthesizing assumed adversarial capabilities, attack success/stealth, trigger transferability, and defense coverage across representative prior works; (3) a structured contrast of two multimodal defenses, CleanCLIP and CleanerCLIP, along granularity, mechanism, adaptive-attack robustness, overhead, and federated/industrial applicability; and (4) an identified research gap -- no existing work addresses backdoor attacks in the sensor/time-series contrastive-learning domain or in federated + industrial deployment settings. The paper runs no original experiments and proposes no new attack or defense.
threat_model: The paper does not define its own threat model; it summarizes the threat models used by the individual surveyed works. As synthesized by the authors: (1) Centralized pretraining (Carlini & Terzis [7]): a weak, black-box adversary who can poison a small fraction (stated as <0.1%) of pretraining samples without access to model parameters or labels, exploiting large-scale web-scraped datasets used for contrastive pretraining. (2) Federated contrastive learning (BAGEL [11]): a weak-to-moderate adversary controlling one or a few malicious clients who follow the training protocol but subtly bias local contrastive updates so the backdoor is embedded in the shared global encoder after aggregation. (3) Multimodal pretraining (CleanCLIP [24], BadCLIP [25]): an adversary who injects a small number of malicious image-text pairs into CLIP-style pretraining data to bias cross-modal alignment. Across all three, the survey notes that because contrastive learning lacks explicit class labels, attacks manipulate representation-space geometry rather than decision boundaries, which the authors argue is what makes label-based/output-based defenses ineffective against this attack family.
limitations: (1) No original experiments: every quantitative and qualitative comparison (Tables I-V) is a re-statement of claims/labels from cited papers, not something the survey authors measured themselves -- entries like 'High (>90%)' or 'Very High (>95%)' attack success rate are coarse buckets the authors assign to prior work's reported numbers, not independently verified figures. (2) Coverage is thin and uneven: some categories (Graph Backdoor Attacks, Section IV-C) get a single sentence and a single citation [23], so the taxonomy's four dimensions are not evenly populated with representative examples. (3) The paper's own prose contains an internal inconsistency about defense readiness -- Table IV lists CleanCLIP's key limitation as a 'performance-robustness tradeoff' while the narrative in Section VI-A also states CleanCLIP is vulnerable to attacks specifically engineered to evade similarity-based filtering, understating that gap in the table summary. (4) The introduction motivates sensor/IoT/wearable contrastive learning as a security-relevant gap but the paper does no analysis of that domain beyond stating it as future work; the 'Analysis' and defense sections do not touch sensor data at all. (5) As a survey, it inherits whatever measurement/threat-model weaknesses exist in the underlying cited papers without independently re-verifying them.

quantitative_results: Carlini & Terzis [7] (cited, not reproduced by this survey's authors): poisoning less than 0.1% of contrastive pretraining data causes downstream classifiers to associate a trigger with an attacker-chosen target label.

Table II (survey's own qualitative bucketing of cited results): Attack Success Rate for Carlini & Terzis [7] = 'High (>90%)' with 'Negligible' clean-accuracy drop; Bi-level Trigger Optimization [10] = 'Very High (>95%)' with 'Negligible' clean-accuracy drop; BAGEL [11] = 'High' ASR with 'Low' clean-accuracy drop; Graph CL Backdoor [23] = 'Moderate-High' ASR with 'Low' clean-accuracy drop; CleanCLIP attack analysis [24] = 'High' ASR with 'Negligible' clean-accuracy drop -- all cross-task-transferable per the table's 'Cross-task Transfer' column (Yes for all but Graph CL Backdoor, marked 'Limited').

CleanerCLIP [26] (cited, not reproduced by this survey's authors): reported to reduce attack success rate from over 90% to 'near-random levels' across patch-based, semantic, and multimodal triggers, with negligible degradation on zero-shot and linear-probe clean performance.
baselines_compared: Poisoning and Backdooring Contrastive Learning (Carlini & Terzis) [7] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
Bi-level Trigger Optimization [10] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
BAGEL (federated contrastive backdoor) [11] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
Graph CL Backdoor [23] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
CleanCLIP [24] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
CleanerCLIP [26] -- surveyed, not benchmarked by this paper
